text: A safe adhesive lysis is very important in any advanced laparoscopic procedures.

If anybody is planning to do ventral hernia repair or if any patients coming even for laparoscopic cholecystectomy, if there is any history of previous multiple surgeries, One should know how to perform safe adhesiolysis.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully release adhesions, demonstrating the technique of adhesiolysis.
The surgeon uses sharp scissors to release adhesions, demonstrating the precision and effectiveness of this instrument in the procedure.
The surgeon applies adequate counter-attraction to expose the plane for adhesiolysis, ensuring clear visualization of the tissue layers for safe dissection.
A little bit of bleeding if it is there, there is no need to be worried. It will get settled automatically. Unless it is a spurter, it is not necessary to control each and every bleeding.
The surgeon applies diathermy to the adhesions, maintaining a safe distance from the bowel to keep the surgical field clean.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, identifying the omentum and noting the presence of bowel immediately behind it. The surgeon exercises caution with each cut to avoid damaging the bowel, which is visibly close to the omentum.

The surgeon releases the omentum adherent to the bowel, revealing no clear plane between the abdominal wall and bowel loop. To prevent intestinal injury, the surgeon dissects one layer above, taking the peritoneum along with the bowel loop. The pre-peritoneal pad of fat is visible, and occasionally a portion of the rectus sheath is removed.
The surgeon safely removes a patch of peritoneum from the abdominal wall, avoiding injury to the bowel during the adhesiolysis procedure.
The surgeon applies counter traction, revealing another loop of bowel immediately adjacent to the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a thirty-degree telescope and sharp scissors to dissect adhesions, maneuvering between the left and right sides of the surgical field to safely separate tissue layers.
The surgeon divides the sac containing omentum during ventral hernia repair, carefully removing the omentum to prevent necrosis and sinus formation within the hernia sac.
The surgeon removes the entire hernia content, revealing a flattened bowel loop.
The surgeon identifies multiple defects in the surgical field, distinguishing between bowel and hernia sac. The hernia sac is visually confirmed and divided with precision.
The surgeon carefully releases the final adhesion, completing the adhesion release process, with visible separation of tissue layers.
